question.text: What is STT (Securities Transaction Tax) and which derivative transactions attract STT?

answer.text: STT is a tax on securities transactions on Indian exchanges — it applies to equity F&O (sale of futures and exercise/sale of options) but NOT to currency or interest rate derivatives
STT applies to all derivative transactions including currency, interest rate, and equity
STT applies only to delivery-based equity transactions, not to any derivatives
STT is a tax collected by RBI on all forex transactions including currency derivatives

explanation.text: **The Logic:** STT is levied under the Securities Transaction Tax Act on transactions in securities on recognized stock exchanges. It applies to equity derivatives: 0.02% on sale of futures, 0.10% on sale of options (on exercise). STT does NOT apply to currency or interest rate derivatives. **The Trap:** Currency derivatives attract CTT (Commodity Transaction Tax) rules or no transaction tax. Interest rate derivatives also don't attract STT. Only equity derivatives do. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch9 covers STT in detail. Series I Ch9 confirms no STT on currency derivatives. Series XIII compares tax treatment across segments. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For equity F&O traders, STT paid allows the income to be treated as non-speculative business income under Section 43(5). This is the linkage between STT and income classification.
